Case Study 02
Opportunity Analysis User Modeling Competitive Benchmarking MVP Framing Metrics

Case Study — The Rapid 10-Step Method I Used to Create User-Centered Apps at Scale

Focus: opportunity analysis, user modeling, competitive benchmarking, MVP framing, metrics definition (HEART + North Star), design systems alignment, dev-ready documentation.

Context

For an extended period, I worked on the continuous release of mobile applications within a high-scale production environment. Managing multiple projects in parallel required a system that enabled speed without compromising quality.

To make this viable, I developed a methodological framework that combined lightweight research, precise functional definition, and informed decision-making from the earliest stages. The goal was to reduce initial uncertainty, accelerate cross-team communication, and maintain consistency throughout the design and development cycle.

After dozens of launches, I realized the challenge wasn't just producing quickly — it was building a process that was sustainable and predictable. This modular system allowed me to structure, within the first few days, all the elements needed to move forward with clarity: understanding the context, identifying the user, defining the product's value, and establishing a solid functional scope.

In this case study, I describe that process and how it enabled us to deliver products consistently, even under high-speed and resource-constrained conditions.

i

All sensitive details have been anonymized according to NDA requirements.

Methods

Below is an overview of the process, which includes the key phases from opportunity identification to launch and development support. This framework was designed to minimize ambiguity and accelerate alignment during the earliest days of each project.

Early-stage steps (initial days)

  • 🔍 1. Market analysis and opportunity detection ↓
  • 👤 2. Definition of the target user ↓
  • 📊 3. Functional competitor benchmark ↓
  • 💡 4. Definition of the value proposition ↓
  • 🚀 5. MVP definition ↓
  • 📈 6. Definition of key metrics ↓
  • 🎨 7. Brand personality and guidelines ↓

Design & Delivery phase

  • 🧩 8. High-fidelity wireframes and functional documentation ↓
  • 📱 9. Final design (High-Fidelity UI) ↓
  • 🔧 10. Handoff & development support ↓
Step 1

Market Analysis and Opportunity Detection

The business team conducted the initial analysis to identify categories and concepts with growth potential. They used tools such as Data.ai, which provide estimates on trends, downloads, and approximate revenue across different app categories.

This initial analysis served to understand:

  • Which app categories were gaining traction
  • Which monetization models performed best
  • Revenue ranges associated with different ranking positions

They also reviewed high-volume keywords, as this metric often correlates with the size of the potential user base and therefore with expected revenue. This analysis served as the foundation for the following phases — user definition, functional framing, and MVP structure.

Step 2

Definition of the Target User

To define the target user, I combined quantitative and qualitative signals, prioritizing speed and reliability within a fast-paced context with limited resources.

Quantitative Data

Market tools such as Data.ai allowed us to obtain an initial snapshot of the type of users interacting with specific app categories, including:

  • Demographic data (age, gender, usage patterns)
  • Geographic distribution (priority markets based on demand and behavioral trends)

In parallel, collaborating with the ASO team was essential to understand real user intent through keyword analysis. A useful practice at this stage was grouping keywords into semantic clusters, which helped identify dominant needs (motivation, purpose, intent) and prioritize them based on search volume. This analysis made it possible to see which user segments were most likely to be interested in the product.

Qualitative Data

Gathering quick qualitative signals can be challenging when working under tight timelines and limited research budgets. For this type of project, I relied on secondary sources that provide actionable insights:

  • Competitor reviews: Useful for detecting pain points (but not wins, due to the frequency of fake positive reviews).
  • Online communities (e.g., Reddit, Quora, Facebook Groups): Real conversations revealing motivations, frustrations, and expectations.
  • Desk research: Articles, comparisons, and user experiences published in specialized blogs.

This material added depth and context to the user profile, complementing quantitative data with emotional and experiential perspectives.

User Definition

Based on all the insights collected, I defined the most relevant user profiles using:

  • Demographic and geographic data (via tools like Data.ai)
  • Primary user goals (inferred from semantic keyword analysis)
  • Key pain points (from reviews and qualitative analysis)
  • Contextual information (desk research and AI-assisted insights)

The goal was not to create static personas, but strategic profiles that could:

  • Enable fast app launches
  • Guide feature prioritization
  • Define MVPs aligned with real user needs
  • Support design and content decisions

When timelines allowed, these profiles were used to conduct in-depth interviews (6–10 users), either internally or through an external agency, enriching our understanding even further.

After launch, real usage data helped update and refine these profiles — closing the loop between initial hypotheses and actual user behavior.

Step 3

Competitor Functional Benchmark

To identify relevant competitors, I used tools like Data.ai along with keyword-based searches directly in the app stores. Once the closest products were identified, I conducted a functional benchmark analyzing their main sections, interaction patterns, and key features.

Building a comparative table was especially useful because it allowed me to distinguish between:

  • Baseline features expected within the category (present in most competitors)
  • Differentiating elements that could add value or support positioning
  • Common functional patterns shaping user experience expectations
  • Areas of inconsistency or friction, which revealed clear opportunities for improvement

This analysis not only provided a quick understanding of the competitive standard, but also served as an operational foundation for the following phases: defining the value proposition, prioritizing features, and structuring the MVP.

Step 4

Defining the Value Proposition

I defined the value proposition using a clear structure:

i

Problem we solve → Target audience → Core benefit → Differentiating element.

Depending on the competitive context, I evaluated whether the product should adopt a follower positioning (aligned with market standards) or a differentiated positioning (filling unmet user needs).

Brainstorming sessions with stakeholders helped validate ideas and identify additional opportunities, which were then incorporated into the comparative table. This strategic foundation guided both the MVP prioritization and the design decisions that followed.

Step 5

Defining the MVP

Once the target users, market context, and value proposition were defined, I moved into a deeper phase: shaping the MVP. I gathered all the functionalities identified during the benchmark and ideation sessions, and defined the scope using the MoSCoW prioritization method:

Must

Essential elements that must be included in the MVP.

Should

High-value features recommended but not critical for initial release.

Could

Optional features to consider for future iterations.

Won't

Features explicitly excluded from the current scope.

This approach enabled the creation of a realistic MVP, aligned with user needs, consistent with the value proposition, and feasible for the development team — avoiding technical overload and reducing ambiguity during planning.

At this stage, I also created initial low-fidelity diagrams and flows, which were extremely useful for communicating the functional scope to stakeholders and ensuring a shared understanding before progressing to more detailed design phases.

i

In some projects, early data indicated comparable user interest in several features. To avoid subjective decisions, we used a value-vs-complexity matrix with the development team to prioritize high-impact, low-effort items.

Step 6

Defining Key Metrics

Once the core functionalities and monetization model were defined, I established the key metrics that would allow us to evaluate the initial performance of each app quickly and consistently. In a fast-paced environment with recurrent launches, we needed a standard framework that provided a clear reading of user behavior without requiring complex analytical implementations.

For this reason, I used the HEART framework, which balances simplicity with diagnostic value:

H
Happiness

Lightweight satisfaction ratings within the main flow. App Store / Google Play ratings as initial indicator.

E
Engagement

Number of images created, edits performed, or weekly sessions — aligned with the value proposition.

A
Adoption

% of new users completing key actions — a critical signal for validating onboarding and functional structure.

R
Retention

Cohort retention (D2, D7, D14, D30), distinguishing subscribed vs non-subscribed users.

T
Task Success

Funnel capturing main paths toward the primary goal — how many users reached the promised value.

In addition to HEART, I defined a North Star Metric for each app, aligned with its business model. In subscription-based products with free tokens, engagement typically proved to be the most reliable predictor of long-term retention and conversion.

i

These metrics not only provided a quick read on product performance but also guided the first iterations — highlighting which apps had potential to scale and where functional blockers needed to be addressed in later releases.

Over time, we developed more detailed dashboards (feature-level conversions, specific funnels, cohort analyses), which enabled more informed decision-making as each product matured.

Step 7

Brand Personality and Guidelines

At this stage of the process — once the concept was defined — I initiated a key phase: aligning the app's identity across all teams. Over time, we learned that establishing the brand personality and guidelines early on was essential for ensuring consistency, both at launch and throughout future iterations.

To achieve this, I organized a joint session with the main stakeholders: Product, UX/UI, Branding, Copy/Content, and ASO (when naming or store positioning were relevant).

Before the workshop, I shared a brief information pack to ensure everyone arrived aligned. It included: user profiles, MVP functionalities, key competitors, product goals, and a simple overview of the session's format.

During the workshop, we explored elements such as:

  • The perceptions the brand should evoke
  • Core concepts and differentiating attributes
  • Tone and style
  • Visual and verbal references
  • Initial naming ideas (aligned with ASO strategy)

Based on the workshop outcomes, each team created its own specific guidelines to ensure coherence:

Content Guidelines
  • Tone of voice definition
  • Writing style and vocabulary
  • Naming conventions
  • Onboarding and in-app microcopy
  • ASO text (title, subtitle, description)
Style Guide (UX/UI)
  • Typography system and scale
  • Color palette and usage rules
  • Component library patterns
  • Spacing, grid, and layout system
  • Iconography and illustration style
Brandbook
  • Brand values and personality traits
  • Logo usage and variations
  • Color identity and combinations
  • Visual language and mood board
  • App store visual assets

These guidelines not only structured internal work, but also served as a reference for other involved teams — Customer Service, Marketing, ASO — ensuring that the app maintained a strong and consistent identity over time.

Step 8

High-Fidelity Wireframes and Functional Documentation

In this phase, I produced the complete functional documentation for each feature, grouping user stories into clear and consistent deliverables. The goal was to reduce ambiguity and ensure that development, content, and QA shared the same understanding of the actual scope.

Each package included:

  • High-fidelity wireframes to clarify interaction and expected behavior
  • Full user flows, covering primary, alternative, and edge paths
  • Functional descriptions of screens and components, including behavior, rules, and constraints
  • Business rules and activation conditions for each functionality
  • States and variables (loading, empty, error, success)
  • Corner cases and exceptional scenarios that could affect implementation
  • Technical dependencies identified during the analysis
  • Interaction specifications — gestures, transitions, and microinteractions

When uncertainties arose around a pattern or when a design decision carried risk, I conducted focused benchmarks or quick tests to validate assumptions before closing the specification.

i

Once validated, this documentation became the single source of truth for design, development, and QA throughout implementation — reducing rework and accelerating delivery in high-speed cycles.

Step 9

Final Design (High-Fidelity UI)

With the functional wireframes approved, the content team completed the final copy, and the UI team developed the definitive screens, incorporating all necessary visual elements: iconography, illustrations, animations, and microinteractions.

During this phase, we also refined interaction details and visual behavior, ensuring consistency with the value proposition and brand guidelines. When questions arose around patterns, visual hierarchy, or specific behaviors, we conducted quick tests to compare alternatives and confidently finalize the version.

Step 10

Handoff & Development Support

During handoff, I supported the development team by resolving functional questions, clarifying rules, reviewing states, behaviors, and dependencies, and ensuring that the implementation adhered to the logic defined in the functional documentation and approved flows.

i

This process allowed us to design and launch applications at scale while maintaining consistency, functional quality, and the speed required to handle multiple products in parallel. Although each app had its own specificities, this framework helped reduce ambiguity, enable fast data-informed decisions, and facilitate smooth collaboration between design, product, and development teams.

My Responsibilities
  • Led the end-to-end product discovery for each new app concept
  • Conducted market opportunity analysis (keywords, trends, demand, competitor coverage)
  • Defined user profiles through demographic, geographic and qualitative insights
  • Mapped ASO keyword strategy to product goals and app structure
  • Benchmarked competitors to identify hygiene features and differentiation points
  • Shaped the value proposition and defined the MVP scope for each app
  • Created low-fidelity flows and structured feature roadmaps for future iterations
  • Defined key metrics (HEART framework + North Star) for launch evaluation
  • Designed high-fidelity wireframes and validated feasibility with development
  • Conducted lightweight usability validations when required
  • Coordinated with cross-functional teams (Design, ASO, Copy, Development, QA)
Learnings & Challenges
01
Standardization was key to scaling quality.
02
Lightweight research can still produce valuable insights.
03
Clear functional documentation drastically reduces rework.
04
Early alignment with ASO improved product-market fit.
05
Cross-functional rituals accelerated decision-making.

Challenges

  • Aligning stakeholders with conflicting priorities and ensuring decisions remained anchored in user and business value rather than opinions.
  • Making strategic calls with incomplete data, high ambiguity, and aggressive timelines while maintaining product coherence.
  • Balancing short-term delivery pressures with long-term scalability to prevent MVP decisions from creating future technical or UX debt.
  • Facilitating cross-functional alignment (product, engineering, marketing/ASO) to define realistic scopes and reduce risk in the earliest stages.
